coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly cobalt-doped carbon quantum dots for spectrofluorometric determination of pregabalin in pharmaceutical capsules
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hamid, Mohamed Ahmed Abdel
Elagamy, Samar H.
Gamal, Aya
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: The misuse of pregabalin has become a significant issue over the last decade. Consequently, there is a growing demand for a sensitive and selective method for its determination. In this study, an eco-friendly cobalt-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been fabricated and applied as nanoprobes for the fluorometric determination of pregabalin. The CQDs were synthesized through mixed doping with non-metallic atoms such as nitrogen and sulfur, and a metal ion, cobaltous ion, via a microwave-assisted method in just 1.5 min. The synthesized Co-NS-CQDs exhibited advantageous characteristics, including rapid response times, compatibility with various pH levels, exceptional detection limits, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity. The Co-NS-CQDs exhibited a high quantum yield (55 %) relative to NS-CQDs (38 %), with blue emissive light at 438 nm. The assessment of pregabalin was based on its enhancement effect on the native fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The proposed method had a good linearity over the range of 25–250 µg/mL, with a limit of detection of 4.17 µg/mL and a limit of quantitation of 12.63 µg/mL, respectively. The prepared NS-CQDs have been successfully applied for the pregabalin determination in pharmaceutical capsules, with excellent % recovery (98–102 %). The greenness of the developed method has been investigated using different greenness metrics, in comparison with the reported RP HPLC method. The greenness characteristics of the method originated from the synthesis of CQDs, utilizing sustainable, readily available, and cost-effective starting materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots, Microwave
Green Analytical Chemistry, Pregabalin, Cobalt
Doping

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Procedure for the determination of PGB in Lyrica® capsules using the prepared Co-NS-CQDs.

a) Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) image of the synthesized Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), b) Histogram representing the data from the TEM image, c) High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the synthesized CQDs, revealing a lattice spacing of 0.31 nm, d) Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum displaying the distinctive peaks of the generated CQDs.
Fluorescence spectra of the prepared CQDs in the presence of mixed with increasing concentrations of PGB.
Studying the greenness using (a) GAPI and (b) AGREE of the proposed Co-NS-CQDs method (left side) and the reference method (right side).
Robustness of the presented Co-NS-CQDs method.
Statistical comparison of the proposed fluorescent approach and the reported method for the assay of PGB in Lyrica®.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The theoretical values of f and t test at p=0.05

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The AES of the proposed Co-NS-CQDs method and the reported HPLC procedure.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly cobalt-doped carbon quantum dots for spectrofluorometric determination of pregabalin in pharmaceutical capsules

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta, Egypt, 31111

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al Salam University, Tanta, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al Salam University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al Salam University, Tanta, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Elgeish Street, the medical campus of Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Elgeish Street, the medical campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The misuse of pregabalin has become a significant issue over the last decade. Consequently, there is a growing demand for a sensitive and selective method for its determination. In this study, an eco-friendly cobalt-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been fabricated and applied as nanoprobes for the fluorometric determination of pregabalin. The CQDs were synthesized through mixed doping with non-metallic atoms such as nitrogen and sulfur, and a metal ion, cobaltous ion, via a microwave-assisted method in just 1.5 min. The synthesized Co-NS-CQDs exhibited advantageous characteristics, including rapid response times, compatibility with various pH levels, exceptional detection limits, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity. The Co-NS-CQDs exhibited a high quantum yield (55 %) relative to NS-CQDs (38 %), with blue emissive light at 438 nm. The assessment of pregabalin was based on its enhancement effect on the native fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The proposed method had a good linearity over the range of 25–250 µg/mL, with a limit of detection of 4.17 µg/mL and a limit of quantitation of 12.63 µg/mL, respectively. The prepared NS-CQDs have been successfully applied for the pregabalin determination in pharmaceutical capsules, with excellent % recovery (98–102 %). The greenness of the developed method has been investigated using different greenness metrics, in comparison with the reported RP HPLC method. The greenness characteristics of the method originated from the synthesis of CQDs, utilizing sustainable, readily available, and cost-effective starting materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Pregabalin (PGB), known by the trade name Lyrica®, is commonly used as an anticonvulsant medication to treat neuropathic pain and as an adjuvant in partial seizures [1]. Its chemical name is [S-[+]-3-isobutyl GABA or (S)-3-(aminomethyl)-5-methylhexanoic acid (Figure S1). PGB is structurally and pharmacologically related to the inhibitory neurotransmitter gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA). This drug is frequently used off-label for various medical conditions, including the management of sleep disorders, alcohol withdrawal syndrome, and bipolar disorder [2]. However, there has been a notable increase in cases of PGB misuse, leading to acute poisoning.

The development of effective analytical methods for the determination of PGB is problematic due to the lack of a chromophore. Different methods for PGB determination have been reported, such as UV–visible spectrophotometric and spectrofluorometric methods after derivatization [3,4]. Additionally, chromatographic [5] and potentiometric methods have been documented [6]. However, these methods are often associated with drawbacks, including their reliance on complex instruments, complicated sample preparation steps, limited throughput, and energy consumption. In contrast, the fluorescence detection method has garnered considerable attention due to its cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, and suitability for rapid analysis. Therefore, the primary objective of this study is to develop a highly sensitive spectrofluorometric method for PGB determination utilizing cobalt, nitrogen, and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (Co-NS-CQDs).

CQDs represent a recent addition to the carbon nanomaterial family, which includes carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, carbon nanofibers, and graphenes [7]. They typically consist of an sp2 hybridized carbon core surrounded by a shell of organic functional groups such as COOH, NH2, OH, SH, or polymer aggregates. CQDs can be synthesized using various chemical precursors including uric acid [8], citric acid [9], methionine [10], and cysteine [11]. Biological precursors such as ginger [12], banana stem [13], coffee [14], milk [15], and eggshell [16] have also been utilized for CQDs synthesis. Carbon dots are typically synthesized using two common techniques: top-down and bottom-up approaches. In the "top-down" process, carbon dots are created through chemical and physical cutting methods, including laser ablation/passivation, chemical oxidation, and electrochemical synthesis [17].

On the other hand, in the "bottom-up" approach, carbon dots are generated from specific molecular precursors under controlled conditions, such as hydrothermal and thermal treatments, and microwave irradiation [18]. This method requires fewer carbon sources and is generally preferred over the "top-down" approach due to several limitations associated with the latter. These limitations include the high cost of starting materials, extended processing time, and the use of harsh conditions

Recently, scientists have been exploring the use of different heteroatoms (N, S, B, P, F) and metals (such as zinc, magnesium, silver, copper, gold, gallium) as dopants to improve the optical properties of CQDs [19–21]. Compared to commonly used heteroatoms, heavy metals are favorable electron donors with bigger atomic radius and have more unoccupied orbitals, which can lead to substantial changes in the electronic structure of CQDs [22]. This alteration affects the HOMO-LUMO energy and the band-gap, influencing the photoluminescent properties of CQDs [23]. Metal-doped CQDs exhibit higher optical absorbance in the visible spectrum due to charge-transfer absorption from metal ions to the carbon structure. Moreover, they can display multicolor emissions such as blue, blue-green, green, and yellow, with a higher quantum yield attributed to improved electron-hole transitions [24]. Co-doped CQDs are prepared by introducing both metals and non-metals into CQDs, resulting in novel materials with improved photoluminescence properties, making them suitable for various applications [25]. In our previous work, we prepared cobalt, nitrogen, and sulfur co-doped CQDs (Co-NS-CQDs) to measure monosodium glutamate (MSG) in instant noodles and chicken cubes [25]. In this study, we extend our work to determining PGB in pharmaceutical capsules. To investigate the role of Co-doping, we also compared the response and characteristics of Co-NS-CQDs with Co-free NS-CQDs prepared under the same conditions. Furthermore, we have discussed how to optimize the amount of Co in the prepared nanoprobe. This study presents a simple, easy, green, and rapid method for PGB assay using these developed Co-NS-CQDs. These findings demonstrate the suitability of these Co-NS-CQDs not only for food analysis of MSG samples, but also for application in quality control laboratories to measure PGB in its dosage forms.

It should be highlighted that the synthesized Co-NS-CQDs offer distinct advantages over non-metal heteroatom-doped carbon dots, particularly NS-CQDs that have been used in various applications over the last years [26]. These advantages arise from the improved physicochemical properties through changes in charge density and transitions between the graphene matrix and metal ions, achieved by the introduction of cobalt, a transition metal atom [27]. This, in turn, results in higher selectivity for PGB and a greater quantum yield. Additionally, compared to some previously reported metal-doped CQDs [28–30], the synthesized mixed-doped CQDs in this study, incorporating cobalt as a metal along with nitrogen and sulfur as non-metal elements, yield materials with enhanced photoluminescence properties and selectivity [25].

Citric acid (99 %) and methionine (99 %) were purchased from Sigma Company (Quesna, Egypt). Hydrochloric acid (98.5 %) and sulfuric acid (laboratory reagent grade) were purchased from Srl Chemicals (Mumbai, India). Quinine sulfate (99 %) was obtained from Alpha Chemika (Mumbai, India) and used as a reference for the quantum yield measurements. Cobalt acetate, NaOH and KI (99 %) were of laboratory grade and supplied from ISO-Chem (Vert-le-petit, France). PGB (99.8 %) was bought from Alfa Aesar (Haverhill, MA, USA). Lyrica®, hard gelatin capsule, containing 75 mg of PGB per capsule and manufactured by Pfizer (Cairo, Egypt), were obtained from Tanta University Hospital Pharmacy.

The fluorescence measurements were carried out using a Jasco FP-6300 Spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) with a slit width of 5 nm at a scanning speed of 1000 nm/min using a version of 1.55 of spectra manager software. The surface functional groups of the prepared CQDs were identified by FTIR (Jasco 4100, Tokyo, Japan). Shimadzu UV-1800 double-beam spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) was used for recording the absorption spectra. Set of analytical techniques were used for CQDs characterization including transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Tokyo, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using K-ALPHA (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA USA) equipped with monochromatic X-ray Al K-alpha radiation −10–1350 eV. The spot size was set at 400 µm, and the measurements were performed at a pressure of 10−9 mbar. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was also utilized for further characterization of CQDs. The chromatographic analysis was performed using Dionex (Sunnyvale, CA, USA) equipped with quaternary RS pump, diode array detector and auto-sampler injector. The chromatographic separation was carried out on Inertsil ODS-3 C18 (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 µm) column. A domestic microwave (1000 watt) was used during the synthesis of CQDs. Other instruments including a hot plate magnetic stirrer (DAIHAN, Shah Alam, Malaysia), digital balance (Sartorius, Goettingen, Germany) and pH-meter equipped with a double junction glass membrane electrode (HANNA, Woonsocket, Rhode Island, USA) were used.

The CQDs were synthesized using a microwave method involving citric acid, methionine, and cobalt acetate as precursors. In a 5 mL flask, 0.06 g of cobalt acetate was dissolved in DW, to make a red solution. In another 5 mL flask, a solution was prepared by dissolving half a gram of citric acid and 0.3 g of methionine in DW. Subsequently, the two solutions were mixed in a ceramic container and transferred to a microwave oven with an 800-watt power rating for 90 seconds, leading to the formation of sticky layer of Co-NS-CQDs. Any residual particles were eliminated through dialysis membrane ( Fig. 1). The as-prepared CQDs were stored in a refrigerator for future use. Fluorescence intensities were monitored at an emission wavelength of 438 nm after excitation at 350 nm against the blank to construct the calibration curve. The blank was prepared by adding half a milliliter of CQDs in a volumetric flask (10 mL) and complete the volume with DW.

The quantum yield (QY) of the prepared Co-NS-CQDs were performed according to a known procedure [31]. The reference compound was used as quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 with QY= 54 %. Then, the QY of CQDs was determined according to the following equation:
Q
CQDs
=
Q
Ref
×
F
CQDs
F
Ref
×
A
Ref
A
CQDs
×
η
CQDs
2
η
Ref
2

Where F indicates the fluorescence intensity after excitation at 350 nm. While A is the absorbance response and η refers to the refractive index of the solvents (η = 1.33 for both CQDs and reference). The subscripts Ref and CQDs refer to quinine sulfate and carbon quantum dots, respectively. Different solutions of quinine sulfate and CQDs were prepared until the absorbance value was below 0.1 at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Then, the fluorescence intensities of both solutions were measured.

A 1 mg/mL stock solution of PGB was prepared by dissolving 0.01 g of PGB in distilled water in a 10 mL flask. Then, various volumes of PGB stock solution, ranging from 250 µL - 2500 µL, were added to 500 µL of the CQDs solution, and the volume was made up to 10 mL with distilled water. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensities were measured at 438 nm after excitation at 350 nm. To create a calibration curve, triplicate preparations of five different PGB concentrations within the range of 25–250 µg/mL were prepared, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded for each concentration.

The contents of ten Lyrica® capsules were emptied and accurately weighed. An amount of powder equivalent to one capsule was carefully weighed, transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask, and completed to the mark with distilled water. The resulting solution was then filtered to produce a stock solution of PGB. A working solution with a concentration of 150 µg/mL was prepared by pipetting 1500 µL of the stock solution into a 10 mL flask, adding 500 µL of the CQDs solution, and completing the volume with distilled water (Fig. 1). Subsequently, the concentrations of PGB were determined using the procedure outlined in Section "2.5." The % recovery and standard deviation values were calculated and compared with the results obtained using HPLC method [32].

The preparation of Co-NS-CQDs presents enhanced physicochemical properties over NS-CQDs, which have found widespread applications in recent years. These enhanced properties could be due to the alternations in charge density and interactions between the graphene matrix and metal ions, facilitated through the incorporation of Co [27]. Consequently, these variations resulted in increased selectivity towards PGB and improved the quantum yield, as summarized in Table S1.

The effect of the cobalt amount on the photophysical properties of the prepared CQDs was investigated. The cobalt amount was varied within the range of 0.01–0.1 g. As depicted in Figure S2, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs exhibited an increase with the rise in cobalt amount, reaching its peak at 0.06 g. However, further increases in cobalt content resulted in a reduction in intensity. This observation can be attributed to the excessive cobalt acetate, which might quench the fluorescence of the synthesized CQDs. Consequently, the optimal amount of cobalt for CQDs synthesis was determined to be 0.06 g.

The characterization of the synthesized CQDs was carried out using various analytical techniques. Initially, TEM was utilized to investigate the morphological structure of the CQDs, revealing the presence of nearly spherical nanoparticles with a polydisperse size distribution ranging between 2 and 10 nanometers Fig. 2a-b. Then, HR-TEM was employed, showing the crystalline nature of the CQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.31 nanometers as shown in Fig. 2c.

Furthermore, FTIR was employed to examine the surface functional groups of the CQDs as shown in Fig. 2d. Notably, peaks in the range of 3200–3500 cm−1 were attributed to the presence of O-H and N-H bonds, indicating the existence of hydroxyl and amino groups. Another peak at 2850 cm−1 was referred to alkyl C-H stretch. Additionally, the appearance of peaks around 1750 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 suggested the presence of CO and CC bonds, respectively. Furthermore, a peak at 2460 cm−1 was assigned to the presence of S-H groups. The observed peak on 1190 cm−1 was due to CS group. These results demonstrated that the surface of CQDs containing functional groups including hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl and thiol groups. The successful doping enhance the stability of CQDs in aqueous system and increase the chelating sites. EDX was performed for further elucidation of the composition of the synthesized CQDs. The results show that the composition of CQDs are C (45.20 %), O (30.56 %), N (11.10 %), S (2.17 %) and Co (10.97 %). The QY of the prepared CQDs was calculated to be 55 % using quinine sulfate as a reference. The CQDs have bright blue fluorescence using UV light. The fluorescence spectra show the highest emission at 438 nm after excitation at 350 nm and their overlay with PGB (Figure S3). It was found that the emission of CQDs was excitation dependent (Figure S4) on excitation with wavelengths in the range of (300–390 nm). Furthermore, XPS was performed for studying the functional groups of the as-prepared CQDs that present on the surface [33]. The full range XPS figure exhibited five distinct peaks at 284.99, 532.07, 400.35, 163.21 and 781.32 eV, which refer to the specific C1s, O1s, N1s, S2p and Co2p, respectively as shown in Figure S5A. The high resolution spectrum of C1s (Figure S5B) revealed three peaks at 284.51, 286.12 and 287.53 eV, which can be ascribed to C-C, C–O and CO, respectively. The HR O1s spectrum (Figure S5C) exhibited three bands at 531.62 and 532.88 eV, which can be referred to O-CO and C-OH, respectively. The HR spectrum for N1s (Figure S5D) showed two bands at 400.03 and 401.61 eV, which can be assigned to C–N–C and N-H, respectively. The HR spectrum of S2p (Figure S5E) displayed two characteristic peaks at 163.4 and 167.1 eV, assigned to C-S-C. The high resolution spectrum for Co2p (Figure S5F) showed two bands at 782.3 and 796.5 eV, which can be ascribed to 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2 of Co3+ and the corresponding bands of Co2+ which were located at 785.3 and 801.5 eV, confirming the successful doping of cobalt atoms into CQDs [34].

In this study, the introduction of cobalt into NS-CQDs resulted in enhanced selectivity for PGB compared to NS-CQDs, which showed no interaction with PGB. This enhanced selectivity in Co-NS-CQDs is attributed to the complex formation between the positively charged cobalt and the carboxylate group of PGB. Additionally the introduction of cobalt resulted in significant changes in the physicochemical properties and characterization features. A detailed comparison of these properties and features between Co-NS-CQDs and NS-CQDs is presented in Table S1.

The influence of salt analysis on the synthesized CQDs was assessed using introducing KI (potassium iodide) at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. The fluorescence behavior of the Co-NS-CQDs, as illustrated in Figure S6, remained unaffected even with the addition of various concentrations of KI, ranging from 25 to 500 µg/mL. This observation indicates that the surface of the CQDs lacks ionization groups that would typically be affected by the presence of salt ions. This exceptional stability in the presence of salt ions suggests that these CQDs are suitable for applications involving harsh conditions.

The effect of incubation time was illustrated to study the interaction between CQDs and PGB. The influence of time was investigated for 15 min within 1 min interval. According to Figure S7, it was found that the reaction between CQDs and PGB was rapid and instantaneously happened and the fluorescence intensities of CQDs remained constant over 15 minutes.

The response of the fluorescence ratio of CQDs and blank plots as a function of CQDs volume in the range of (125–1500 µL). As shown in Figure S8, it was found that increasing the amounts of Co-NS-CQDs will decrease the fluorescence ratio. This phenomenon can be rationalized by the tendency of CQDs to aggregate at higher volumes, which can attenuate their fluorescence. As a result, it was determined that the optimum volume of CQDs is 500 µL.

The effect of pH on the developed CQDs was investigated over the range 1–10. The pH is a critical factor as it affects the ionization and solubility of analytes (Figure S9). At acidic conditions, specifically in the pH range of 1–3, the fluorescence intensities were notably low. This can be attributed to the self-assembling aggregation of CQDs and the formation of hydrogen bonds between surface oxygen groups and CQDs. Additionally, the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups present on the surface of CQDs may undergo protonation at these low pH values. However, as the pH was adjusted to the range of 4–8, the fluorescence intensities were relatively consistent. The pH 6 was selected for optimal fluorescence response.

The designed method was carefully validated regarding to ICH guidelines. Different parameters were considered including linearity, range, limit of detection, limit of quantitation, accuracy, precision and robustness. The obtained results were found to be analytically acceptable.

A linear correlation was established when the fluorescence intensity was plotted against the concentration of PGB within the 25–250 µg/mL range. Linearity was achieved by using the average of the results obtained from triplicate analysis of the standard solutions of six different concentrations ( Fig. 3). The method's validity was demonstrated by a statistical evaluation of the regression lines. The high coefficient of determination value demonstrates strong linearity of the method within the stated concentration range (Table S2).

The limits of detection (LOD) and limits of quantification (LOQ) were calculated using the calibration curve according to the following formulas: The equation LOQ = 10 σ/S represents the limit of quantification, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank and S represents the slope of the calibration line. Similarly, the equation LOD = 3.3 σ/S represents the limit of detection. The calculated LOQ and LOD were 12.0 μg/mL and 4.0 μg/mL, respectively.

In order to assess the precision of the suggested approach, three distinct concentrations within the linear range (25, 50, 150, 250 µg/mL) were tested. Three replicates of each concentration were made and the percentage of recovery was calculated. Table S3 demonstrates that the percentage recoveries fell within the range of 98–102 %, showing the method's accuracy.

The reliability of the devised analytical approach was proven by the results of intra-day and inter-day precision testing. To assess the intra-day precision, three distinct concentrations of PGB within the linear range were tested three times on the same day. To assess the inter-day precision, the identical procedures were repeated over the course of three consecutive days. The precision of the approaches can be determined by examining the values of % relative standard deviation, as presented in Table S4. Acceptable precision is indicated by values below 2 %.

The robustness was demonstrated by evaluating the influence of deliberate minor changes in the optimization variables as pH (6± 0.5), emission wavelength (438± 5), excitation wavelength (350± 5) and incubation time. To assess the effect of measurement conditions on the performance of the method, one parameter was modified while maintaining the remaining parameters at their optimal states. Table 1 illustrates the outcomes in terms of percentage recovery, SD, and %relative SD (%RSD). The obtained results unequivocally indicate the remarkable robustness of the developed method, as it withstood minor variations in these parameters without significant changes, exhibiting % RSD values of less than 2 %.

The proposed analytical method was employed to determine PGB within Lyrica® capsules. The % recovery was calculated based on the average of six determinations of fluorescence intensities of a concentration of 150 µg/mL. These results were statistically compared to the reference HPLC method using F-test and t-test at a 95 % confidence level, and the results are summarized in Table 2. The tests revealed no notable disparity between the two approaches, therefore affirming the dependability of the suggested procedure for assessing PGB in pharmaceutical formulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green assessment of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: Green Analytical Chemistry (GAC) has gained recent attention as a means to reduce the negative environmental and health impacts associated with analytical procedures. GAC employs green chemistry metrics to assess the safety of analytical methods, such as the Analytical Eco Scale (AES), Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI), and AGREE [35]. The AES is a semi-quantitative tool that evaluates analytical procedures by considering factors such as chemical hazards, energy consumption, waste generation, and occupational risks. It assigns penalty points to each aspect, deducts the total penalty points from 100, and classifies methods as excellent green (score above 75) or acceptable green (score above 50). GAPI provides a more detailed assessment, covering aspects from sample collection to the final determination. It uses five pentagrams in green, yellow, or red to indicate low, medium, or high environmental impact, respectively. AGREE, an open-source software, evaluates greenness based on twelve GAC principles. It assigns a score from 0 to 1, represented in a circular pictogram with sections of varying colors, reflecting the environmental impact. The proposed method in this study surpasses the reported HPLC method in terms of greenness, as indicated by the results of the Eco Scale Table 3, GAPI and AGREE Fig. 4. This is mainly attributed to the use of environmentally friendly solvents like water and the fact that the method does not require any special treatment or generate any organic waste, unlike the HPLC method.

The doping of carbon quantum dots has been granted significant attention due to several inherent advantages. These advantages include the use of cost-effective carbon sources, good water solubility, straightforward surface functionalization, and high quantum yield. The introduction of both metal and non-metal ion dopants has expanded the utility of CQDs in a wide variety of applications. In this study, Co-NS-CQDs were efficiently synthesized using a simple and rapid microwave-assisted method, taking less than two min. The native fluorescence of CQDs exhibited a linear increase in intensity with the addition of PGB within a concentration range of 25–250 µg/mL. This method proved to be highly accurate, precise, sensitive, and selective for the determination of PGB in pharmaceutical dosage forms. An advantageous aspect of the proposed CQDs is their capability to directly detect PGB without the need for chemical derivatization or the use of complex instrumentation. This enables the routine analysis of PGB in bulk materials and pharmaceutical preparations with simplicity and efficiency. Additionally, the synthesized Co-NS-CQDs show promise as fluorescent nanoprobe for further practical applications in pharmaceutical and environmental analysis.
No fund was received for this research.

Mohamed Ahmed Abdel Hamid: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Samar H. Elagamy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Aya Gamal: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fotouh Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jpba.2024.116270.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric and spectrofluorimetric methods for the determination of pregabalin in bulk and pharmaceutical preparation
Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Önal, O. Sagirli, Spectrophotometric and spectrofluorimetric methods for the determination of pregabalin in bulk and pharmaceutical preparation, Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc. 72 (2009) 68–71. doi:10.1016/j.saa.2008.08.009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of gabapentin and pregabalin on calcium homeostasis: implications for physical rehabilitation of musculoskeletal tissues
Curr. Osteoporos. Rep.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.C. Reyes Fernandez, C.S. Wright, S.J. Warden, J. Hum, M.C. Farach-Carson, W.R. Thompson, Effects of Gabapentin and Pregabalin on Calcium Homeostasis: Implications for Physical Rehabilitation of Musculoskeletal Tissues, Curr. Osteoporos. Rep. 20 (2022) 365–378. doi:10.1007/s11914-022-00750-x.
